meta.title: 10.1 Rate Limiting Algorithms
meta.description: Token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window. Distributed rate limiting and client-side strategies.

blocks.prompt: Your API allows 100 requests per minute per user. At minute boundaries (e.g., 12:00:59 and 12:01:00), a user sends 100 requests in each window — 200 requests in 2 seconds. Is your rate limiter broken?

blocks.content:
## Rate limiting algorithms

### Token Bucket
A bucket holds tokens (max = burst size). Tokens added at a fixed rate. Each request consumes one token. If empty, request is rejected. Allows bursts up to bucket size.

### Leaky Bucket
Requests enter a queue (bucket). Processed at a fixed rate. If queue is full, new requests are rejected. Smooths bursts to a constant rate.

### Fixed Window
Count requests in fixed time windows (e.g., per minute). Reset counter at window boundary. Problem: burst at window edges (your predictPrompt scenario — 200 requests in 2 seconds).

### Sliding Window
Combines fixed window efficiency with sliding accuracy. Weight the previous window's count by overlap percentage.

```
Current window: 12:01, 30 seconds in (50% through)
Previous window (12:00): 80 requests
Current window (12:01): 30 requests so far

Estimate: 80 × 0.5 + 30 = 70 requests
Limit: 100 → Allow
```

| Algorithm | Burst handling | Memory | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Token bucket | Allows controlled bursts | Low | Good |
| Leaky bucket | Smooths to constant rate | Low | Good |
| Fixed window | Edge burst problem | Low | Moderate |
| Sliding window | No edge bursts | Medium | High |

### Distributed rate limiting
With multiple API servers, each needs to check the same counter. Use Redis with atomic INCR + EXPIRE. Or use a local rate limiter with a global sync (less accurate, more resilient).

Token bucket allows controlled bursts; leaky bucket enforces constant rate; sliding window is the most accurate. For distributed systems, use Redis-backed counters. Return HTTP 429 with Retry-After header.

blocks.prompt: Rate limiting prevents overload from external traffic. Module 10.2 covers circuit breakers — preventing cascade failures between internal services.
